title: Add image cooldown/Minimum Release Age setting
body:
### Feature request description

A a image cooldown feature to enforces a configurable minimum image age before podman auto-updater will apply an update to a container. By requiring that an image has existed in the registry for a certain period of time, the cooldown provides a defense against supply-chain attacks, where a compromised image is published and immediately pulled by automated update tools. In addition, this helps to reduce the chance of receiving potentially buggy updates prior to the developer releasing an emergency patch.

### Suggest potential solution

It should be possible to configure this cooldown period globally or per container.

### Have you considered any alternatives?

No

### Additional context

References in other tools:
* https://watchtower.nickfedor.com/v1.16.1/configuration/arguments/#cooldown_delay
* https://docs.renovatebot.com/key-concepts/minimum-release-age/#what-is-minimum-release-age

comments.body:
> provides a defense against supply-chain attacks, where a compromised image is published and immediately pulled by automated update tools.

That is impossible, AFAIK the registries do not expose any kind of publish date information. The creation date you see in podman images is embedded in the image config so controlled by the attacker if they can push new images.

Therefore this adds no protection against malicious pushes and hence I do not think this feature would be useful.

It would likely better to design your workflow in some gitopts style where you pin all images by digest instead of tags and then have something like renovate update your image digest based on new releases and then use its minimum age option there.

Perhaps the cooldown timer can use its own timestamps of when it saw the images on the repository?

For example, suppose we update once per day, and set a minimum cooldown of 2 days.

1. On day 1, we see a new image A. This starts the cooldown timer.
2. On day 2, A is still the newest image.
3. On day 3, B is the newest image, but image A still exists. The cooldown timer for A has expired, so we pull A. We start another cooldown timer for image B.

This is not very granular, e.g. when auto-updating once per day, the least meaningful cooldown is 1 day. But it avoids the problem of having attacker-controlled timestamps.

The problem is that this requires the command to become stateful which sure is technically possible but would also make it much more complex. podman auto-update is a generic command, there is no requirement to use the auto-update.timer as you can run on the cli whenever you want.

Second the way you describe it I am not sure what you gain other than delayed breakages. Tags are not static so what we would pin is the digest. AFAIK most registries do not remove old digests (but some could), so then per your example on day 3 we pull by digest which is still the same thing. So if there are critical bugs or security issues in there you now just got them 2 days later with no way to prevent consuming them via auto-update logic.

Really like I said above this sounds like a workflow issue that is not solvable via a simple podman command. Use some gitops (or similar) style of config management and declare all the images by digest. Then you are in control whenever to update to that digest or not by merging/not merging that PR updating that digest.
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.

> > provides a defense against supply-chain attacks, where a compromised image is published and immediately pulled by automated update tools.
> 
> That is impossible, AFAIK the registries do not expose any kind of publish date information. The creation date you see in podman images is embedded in the image config so controlled by the attacker if they can push new images.
> 
> Therefore this adds no protection against malicious pushes and hence I do not think this feature would be useful.
> 
> It would likely better to design your workflow in some gitopts style where you pin all images by digest instead of tags and then have something like renovate update your image digest based on new releases and then use its minimum age option there.

It is nevertheless a useful feature, as it allows you to skip versions of images for which reputable maintainers provide hotfixes within N days, whether for stability or security reasons.

> It is nevertheless a useful feature, as it allows you to skip versions of images for which reputable maintainers provide hotfixes within N days, whether for stability or security reasons.

No not at all, auto-update would means ALL updates are help back, so now you get your 6.0.0 update  a few days later even when 6.0.1 hotfix is already out per the proposed logic here. So you gain no stability whatsoever and security wise it is much worse since you now delay as security updates.

IMO this is impossible to solve for a single auto-update command, you are looking for proper gitops with pinning images bz digest, the reason it works there is because you can actually configure things and merge updates sooner or later (when you need to hold back updates), etc...

Also I must stress that auto-update does nothing special, it does the exact same thing as podman pull $IMAGE && systemctl restart service. So auto-update is unable to pin an old image because another external pull and service restart might update the image accidentally. Again something that would not happen if you use pinned digest images and to update you update the quadlet file image digest.
